title_en: Treatise on Water, Heat, and the Acupuncture Points
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Why does the lesser yin govern the kidney, and why does the kidney govern water? Qibo replied: The kidney is the utmost yin; the utmost yin is that which abounds in water. The lung is the greater yin, and the lesser yin is the vessel of winter; thus its root lies in the kidney and its branch lies in the lung, both being places where water accumulates. Huangdi asked: How is it that the kidney can gather water and thereby give rise to disease? Qibo replied: The kidney is the gate of the stomach. When the gate fails to open properly, water gathers and follows its own kind, overflowing above and below into the skin, thus producing swelling. Swelling is disease born of gathered water.

Huangdi asked: Do all forms of water disease arise from the kidney? Qibo replied: The kidney is a yin viscus; the qi of earth that rises upward belongs to the kidney and gives rise to water and fluid, hence it is called the utmost yin. When one exerts great force and labors to excess, the kidney sweats. If this kidney sweat encounters wind, the wind can neither enter inward into the viscera and bowels nor pass outward beyond the skin; it lodges instead in the mysterious gateways, travels within the skin, and is transmitted into swelling. Since it is rooted in the kidney, this is called wind water. What are called the mysterious gateways are the pores through which sweat passes.

Huangdi asked: What do the fifty-seven points for treating water disease govern? Qibo replied: The fifty-seven kidney points are where accumulated yin gathers, the places through which water passes in and out. The five rows of five points above the sacrum are the kidney transport points. In water disease, below there is swelling and abdominal distension, and above there is panting and gasping with inability to lie down; this is disease of both branch and root together, for the lung produces the panting and gasping, the kidney produces the water swelling, and the lung's rebellious qi prevents reclining. Where the two are distinguished yet mutually transmit and both are affected, this is where the water qi lodges. The two rows of five points above the crouching-rabbit point are the kidney's thoroughfare, where the three yin channels of the leg meet and knot. The single rows of six points above the ankle are where the kidney vessel descends; this is called the greater surge. In all, these fifty-seven points are the yin network vessels of the viscera, the places where water lodges as a guest.

Huangdi asked: Why in spring does one needle the network vessels and the interstices between the flesh? Qibo replied: In spring, wood begins to govern and the liver qi begins to be generated; the liver qi is urgent and its wind swift, while the channels and vessels remain deep and their qi scant, unable to penetrate deeply—hence one needles the network vessels and the spaces between the flesh. Huangdi asked: Why in summer does one needle the abundant channels and the interstices of the skin? Qibo replied: In summer, fire begins to govern and the heart qi begins to grow; the vessels are slender and the qi weak, while yang qi lingers and overflows, its heat steaming the interstices and penetrating inward to the channels—hence one needles the abundant channels and the skin interstices, cutting through the skin so that the disease departs, for the pathogen dwells only shallowly. What is called the abundant channel is the yang vessel. Huangdi asked: Why in autumn does one needle the transport and connecting points of the channels? Qibo replied: In autumn, metal begins to govern; the lung is about to gather in and bring things to a close, metal is about to overcome fire, yang qi resides at the uniting points, and yin qi has just begun to prevail. Dampness reaches the body, but since yin qi is not yet abundant it cannot penetrate deeply—hence one selects the transport points to drain yin pathogens, and the uniting points to deplete yang pathogens; as yang qi begins to decline, one therefore selects the uniting points. Huangdi asked: Why in winter does one needle the well and spring points? Qibo replied: In winter, water begins to govern, the kidney is closing up, yang qi is diminished and weak, yin qi is firm and abundant, the great yang lies hidden and sunken, and the yang vessels have departed—hence one selects the well points to bring down rebellious yin, and the spring points to fortify yang qi. Thus it is said: needle the well and spring points in winter so that in spring there will be no nosebleed—this is what is meant.

Huangdi asked: Master, you have spoken of the fifty-nine points for treating heat disease; I have considered their meaning but have not yet been able to distinguish their locations. I wish to hear of their locations, and through that to hear their meaning. Qibo replied: The five rows of five points atop the head serve to disperse the rebellious heat of the various yang channels. The eight points of the great shuttle, the chest transport points, the empty basin, and the back transport points serve to drain the heat within the chest. The eight points of the qi thoroughfare, the leg three li, and the upper and lower great hollows serve to drain the heat within the stomach. The eight points of the cloud gate, the shoulder bone, the bend-middle, and the marrow hollow serve to drain the heat of the four limbs. The ten points flanking the five transport points of the five viscera serve to drain the heat of the five viscera. In all, these fifty-nine points are the points to either side that govern heat. Huangdi asked: When a person is injured by cold, and it is transmitted and transformed into heat, why is this? Qibo replied: When cold is abundant, it gives rise to heat.

Huangdi asked: I have heard it said in the methods of needling that in cases of excess one should drain, and in cases of deficiency one should supplement. What is meant by excess, and what is meant by deficiency? Qibo replied: There are five kinds of excess, and likewise five kinds of deficiency. What does Your Majesty wish to ask? Huangdi said: I wish to hear of them all. Qibo said: Spirit may be in excess or deficient; qi may be in excess or deficient; blood may be in excess or deficient; form may be in excess or deficient; and will may be in excess or deficient. These ten conditions all involve qi that is unequal. Huangdi said: A person possesses essence, qi, and fluids, the four limbs and nine orifices, the five viscera and sixteen regions, and the three hundred sixty-five joints, from which the hundred diseases are born. And in the birth of the hundred diseases there is always deficiency or excess. Now Master, you speak of five kinds of excess and five kinds of deficiency—how do these arise? Qibo said: They all arise from the five viscera. The heart stores the spirit, the lung stores qi, the liver stores blood, the spleen stores the flesh, and the kidney stores the will; from these the bodily form is completed. Will and intention penetrate within and connect with the marrow and bones, thereby completing the body's form and the five viscera. The pathways of the five viscera all issue from the channel passages, through which blood and qi travel. When blood and qi are not harmonious, the hundred diseases arise through transformation—therefore one must guard the channel passages.

Huangdi asked: What of spirit in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When spirit is in excess, one laughs without cease; when spirit is deficient, one grieves. Before blood and qi have combined with the pathogen and the five viscera remain settled, if a pathogen lodges in the body it produces a shivering that arises from the fine hairs, not yet having entered the channels and network vessels—this is called a subtle affection of the spirit. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain in this case? Qibo said: When spirit is in excess, drain the blood of its small network vessels; let blood out, but do not press too deeply, and do not strike the great channel—then the spirit qi will be leveled. When spirit is deficient, observe its empty network vessels, press upon them to draw qi there, needle to free them, but do not let blood out, do not drain the qi—so as to open the channel, and the spirit qi will be leveled. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Massage without release, insert the needle without dislodging it, and move qi to where it is deficient—then the spirit qi can be restored.

Huangdi said: Good. What of qi in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When qi is in excess there is panting and coughing with qi rising upward; when qi is deficient, breathing is uneven and qi is scant. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, there is a slight affection of the skin, called a subtle leakage of white qi. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When qi is in excess, drain its channel passage, but do not injure the channel, do not let blood out, do not drain the qi. When deficient, supplement its channel passage, but do not let the qi escape. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Massage without release; when withdrawing the needle, observe it, saying, 'I shall insert it deeply'; once it truly enters, the response will surely change—the essential qi will naturally settle, the pathogenic qi will scatter and disperse with nowhere to rest, the qi will leak out through the interstices, and the true qi will thereby find its proper place.

Huangdi said: Good. What of blood in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When blood is in excess there is anger; when deficient there is fear. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, if the grandchild network vessels overflow with fluid, then there is retained blood in the channel. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When blood is in excess, drain its abundant channel and let out its blood. When deficient, observe its empty channel and insert the needle within its vessel, retaining it long while observing; when the vessel becomes full, withdraw the needle quickly, without letting blood escape. Huangdi asked: How does one needle retained blood? Qibo said: Observe its blood network vessels, needle to let out the blood, and do not let malign blood enter the channel, lest it become a chronic ailment.

Huangdi said: Good. What of form in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When form is in excess there is abdominal distension and difficulty in urination and defecation; when deficient, the four limbs cannot be used. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, the flesh twitches faintly—this is called subtle wind. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When form is in excess, drain its yang channel; when deficient, supplement its yang network vessel. Huangdi asked: How does one needle a subtle case? Qibo said: Select the spaces between the flesh, without striking the channel and without injuring the network vessel—then the defensive qi can be restored and the pathogenic qi will disperse.

Huangdi said: Good. What of will in excess or deficiency? Qibo said: When will is in excess there is abdominal distension and undigested diarrhea; when deficient there is reversal cold. Before blood and qi have combined and the five viscera remain settled, there is stirring in the bones and joints. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement or drain? Qibo said: When will is in excess, drain the blood at Rangu; when deficient, supplement at Fuliu. Huangdi asked: How does one needle when blood and qi have not yet combined? Qibo said: Needle it at once, without striking the channel, and the place of the pathogen can immediately be made deficient.

Huangdi said: Good. I have now heard the forms of deficiency and excess, but do not know how they arise. Qibo said: When qi and blood have combined with a pathogen, yin and yang tip against each other; qi becomes disordered in the defensive level, blood rebels within the channels, and blood and qi become separated from their proper places—one becomes excess, the other deficient. When blood combines in the yin and qi combines in the yang, this produces fright and mania. When blood combines in the yang and qi combines in the yin, this produces a burning within. When blood combines above and qi combines below, there is vexation in the heart and a tendency to anger. When blood combines below and qi combines above, there is confusion and a tendency to forgetfulness. Huangdi asked: When blood combines in the yin and qi combines in the yang, and thus blood and qi are separated from their places, which is excess and which is deficient? Qibo said: Blood and qi delight in warmth and are averse to cold; when cold, they congeal and cannot flow; when warm, they dissolve and disperse. Therefore, wherever qi combines, blood becomes deficient there; wherever blood combines, qi becomes deficient there. Huangdi said: What a person possesses is only blood and qi. Now Master, you say that where blood combines there is deficiency, and where qi combines there is deficiency—is there then no excess at all? Qibo said: Where something is present, that is excess; where something is absent, that is deficiency. Thus where qi combines there is no blood, and where blood combines there is no qi. Now, since blood and qi have lost each other, this is called deficiency. The network vessels and grandchild vessels all convey into the channels; when blood and qi combine there, this becomes excess. When blood and qi together rush upward, this produces great reversal; in reversal there is sudden death—if the qi returns, the person lives; if it does not return, the person dies. Huangdi asked: By what path does excess arrive, and by what path does deficiency depart? I wish to hear the essentials of deficiency and excess. Qibo said: Yin and yang each have their transport points and meeting places. Yang pours into yin, and yin fills to the outside; when yin and yang are evenly balanced, the body is thereby nourished, and the nine indicators are as one—this is called a balanced person.

Now, in the generation of pathogens, some are generated in the yin, some in the yang. Those generated in the yang are contracted through wind, rain, cold, and summer heat. Those generated in the yin are contracted through diet, dwelling, and excesses of yin and yang or joy and anger. Huangdi asked: How does injury from wind and rain affect a person? Qibo said: When wind and rain injure a person, they first lodge in the skin, then are transmitted inward to the grandchild vessels; when the grandchild vessels are full, they are transmitted into the network vessels; when the network vessels are full, they pour into the great channel vessels. Blood and qi combine with the pathogen and lodge between the interstices of the flesh, so that the vessel becomes firm and large—hence it is called excess. In excess, the exterior is firm and full, and cannot bear pressing; pressing produces pain. Huangdi asked: How does injury from cold and dampness affect a person? Qibo said: When cold and dampness strike a person, the skin fails to contract, the flesh becomes tight and firm, the nutritive blood congeals, and the defensive qi departs—hence it is called deficiency. In deficiency, the flesh is wrinkled and sunken, the qi insufficient; pressing on it allows the qi to warm it, hence it feels pleasant and is not painful. Huangdi said: Good. How does excess arise in the yin? Qibo said: When joy and anger are not moderated, yin qi rebels upward; when it rebels upward, the lower part becomes deficient; when the lower part is deficient, yang qi rushes into it—hence it is called excess. Huangdi asked: How does deficiency arise in the yin? Qibo said: Joy causes qi to descend, grief causes qi to dissipate; when qi dissipates, the vessel becomes empty and hollow. When this is compounded by cold food and drink, cold qi steams and fills the interior, the blood congeals and the qi departs—hence it is called deficiency.

Huangdi asked: The classics say that when yang is deficient there is external cold, when yin is deficient there is internal heat, when yang is in excess there is external heat, and when yin is in excess there is internal cold. I have already heard this, but do not know why it is so. Qibo said: Yang receives its qi from the upper burner, which warms the skin and the space between the flesh. If cold qi lodges in the exterior, then the upper burner becomes obstructed; when the upper burner is obstructed, cold qi alone remains in the exterior—hence there is cold and shivering. Huangdi asked: How does deficiency of yin give rise to internal heat? Qibo said: When there has been overexertion and fatigue, the bodily form and qi decline and diminish, grain qi is not abundant, the upper burner fails to move, and the lower stomach-duct fails to open; the stomach qi becomes heated, and this heat steams the chest—hence there is internal heat. Huangdi asked: How does excess of yang give rise to external heat? Qibo said: When the upper burner is obstructed and fails to flow freely, the skin becomes dense and the interstices close up, the mysterious gateways fail to open, and the defensive qi cannot be released and dispersed—hence there is external heat. Huangdi asked: How does excess of yin give rise to internal cold? Qibo said: When reversal qi rebels upward, cold qi accumulates in the chest without being drained; if it is not drained, warm qi departs and cold alone remains; the blood then congeals, and once congealed the vessels fail to flow freely—the vessel becomes large and rough, hence there is internal cold.

Huangdi asked: When yin and yang combine with pathogens, when blood and qi combine, and the form of disease is thereby completed, how does one needle this? Qibo said: In needling such cases, select the channel passages; draw blood from the nutritive level, draw qi from the defensive level, and make use of the bodily form, according to the season, whether abundant or scant, high or low. Huangdi asked: When blood and qi have combined and the form of disease has been completed, and yin and yang tip against each other, how does one supplement and drain? Qibo said: To drain excess, insert the needle once the qi is abundant; let the needle enter together with the qi, opening its gate as though loosening its door; let the needle withdraw together with the qi, so that the essential qi is not injured and the pathogenic qi is thereby brought down. Do not close the outer gate, so as to let the ailment out; shake wide its passage, as though clearing its path—this is called great draining; one must press firmly as the needle is withdrawn, so that the great pathogenic qi is thereby subdued. Huangdi asked: How does one supplement deficiency? Qibo said: Hold the needle without placing it yet, to steady one's intent; await the exhalation and insert the needle; as the qi goes out, the needle goes in; let the needle's hollow be sealed on all four sides, so that the essential qi has nowhere to escape; when it becomes truly replete, withdraw the needle quickly; as the qi enters, the needle comes out, so that heat cannot return; close its gate, so that the pathogenic qi is dispersed and the essential qi may thereby be preserved. Move the qi and await the right moment, so that the near qi is not lost and the distant qi will then arrive—this is called pursuing it.

Huangdi asked: Master, you have spoken of ten kinds of deficiency and excess, arising from the five viscera—the five viscera having five vessels. Yet the twelve channel vessels all give rise to disease; now Master, you speak only of the five viscera. The twelve channel vessels all connect with the three hundred sixty-five joints; when a joint is diseased, it must affect the channel vessel, and the diseases of the channel vessels all involve deficiency or excess—how are these to be reconciled? Qibo said: The five viscera are paired in exterior-interior relation with the six bowels; the channels, network vessels, and joints each give rise to their own deficiency or excess, and one regulates according to where the disease resides. If the disease is in the vessel, regulate the blood. If the disease is in the blood, regulate the network vessels. If the disease is in the qi, regulate the defensive level. If the disease is in the flesh, regulate the spaces between the flesh. If the disease is in the sinews, regulate the sinews. If the disease is in the bone, regulate the bone. Use fire-heated needles for rapid piercing where the disease lies and where there is tension. If the disease is in the bone, use the tempered needle together with medicinal fomentation. If the disease's location of pain cannot be identified, the two springing vessels are best to treat. If the body has pain but the nine indicators show no disease, then apply crossed needling. If the pain is on the left but the affected vessel is diseased on the right, apply great needling. One must carefully examine the nine indicators, and the way of needling will then be complete.

Huangdi asked: I have heard of crossed needling but have not grasped its meaning—what is meant by crossed needling? Qibo replied: When a pathogen lodges in the body, it must first reside in the skin and hair; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the grandchild vessels; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the network vessels; if it lingers there without departing, it enters and resides in the channel vessels, connecting inwardly with the five viscera and dispersing into the intestines and stomach, so that yin and yang are both affected and the five viscera are thereby injured. This is the sequence by which a pathogen enters from the skin and hair to reach its utmost in the five viscera; in such a case one treats the channels. Now, if a pathogen lodges in the skin and hair and enters to reside in the grandchild network vessels, lingering there without departing, it becomes blocked and fails to pass through; unable to enter the channels, it overflows into the great network vessels and gives rise to unusual diseases. When a pathogen lodges in the great network vessels, it pours from the left to the right, from the right to the left, above and below, left and right, interfering with the channels and spreading to the four extremities; its qi has no fixed location and does not enter the channel transport points—this is called crossed needling. Huangdi asked: I wish to hear of crossed needling—of taking the right to treat the left, and the left to treat the right. How is this done, and how does it differ from great needling? Qibo said: When a pathogen lodges in the channel, if the left is abundant, the right will be diseased; if the right is abundant, the left will be diseased—there are also cases where it shifts, where the pain on the left has not yet ceased before the vessel on the right first becomes diseased. In such cases, one must apply great needling, striking precisely the channel, not the network vessel. Thus in network-vessel disease, the pain occurs at a place crossed from the affected channel—hence it is called crossed needling.

Huangdi asked: I wish to hear how crossed needling is performed, and how the points are selected. Qibo said: When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yin, it causes sudden heart pain and abrupt distension, with fullness extending through the chest and rib-sides, without any palpable accumulation. Needle in front of the Rangu point to let blood, and after about the time it takes to eat a meal, it will cease. If it does not cease, take the right side for a left-side ailment, or the left for a right-side ailment. If the disease is newly arisen, it will resolve within five days.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand lesser yang, it causes throat blockage and a curled tongue, dry mouth, and vexation of the heart, with pain along the outer border of the arm such that the hand cannot reach the head. Needle above the nail of the ring finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each hand. In the robust, it resolves at once; in the elderly, it takes a little while. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; this, being a recently arisen disease, resolves within a few days.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot reverting yin, it causes sudden hernial pain of great severity. Needle above the nail of the big toe, at the junction with the flesh, one prick on each side. In males it resolves at once; in females it takes a little while. Take the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yang, it causes pain in the head, neck, and shoulder. Needle above the nail of the little toe, at the junction with the flesh, one prick on each side; it resolves at once. If it does not resolve, needle three times below the outer ankle. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to eat a meal.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand yang brightness, it causes fullness of qi, panting in the chest, with the rib-sides supported and heat in the chest. Needle above the nail of the index finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each side. Take the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to eat a meal.

When a pathogen lodges between the forearm and palm so that it cannot be bent, needle behind the wrist; first press with the finger where it is painful, then needle there, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks: on the first day of the moon's waxing, one prick; on the second day, two pricks; up to the fifteenth day, fifteen pricks; on the sixteenth day, fourteen pricks, and so on decreasing. When a pathogen lodges in the foot yang springing vessel, it causes eye pain beginning from the inner canthus. Needle about half a cun below the outer ankle, two pricks on each side; needle the right for the left, the left for the right; it resolves after about the time it takes to walk ten li. When a person has fallen or been thrown down, so that malign blood remains within, with fullness and distension in the abdomen and inability to relieve oneself, first give a purgative medicine to disperse it. If this fall has injured the reverting yin vessel above and the lesser yin network vessel below, needle below the inner ankle, at the blood vessel in front of Rangu, to let out blood; needle also the pulsing vessel atop the instep. If this does not resolve it, needle above the cluster of hairs near the great toe, one prick on each side; once blood appears, it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right. For one prone to sorrow, fright, and unhappiness, needle according to the method just given.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the hand yang brightness, it causes deafness, so that at times no sound is heard. Needle above the nail of the index finger, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip, one prick on each side; hearing is restored at once. If it does not resolve, needle at the junction of the middle finger's nail with the flesh, and hearing is restored at once. In cases where hearing was never present at all, needling is of no avail. For those in whom wind arises within the ear, needle in the same manner as above, the right for the left, the left for the right.

In all cases of painful obstruction that comes and goes without fixed location, needle wherever the pain occurs in the spaces between the flesh, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks. In wielding the needle, follow the abundance or decline of the qi to determine the number of pricks; if the needling exceeds the proper count of days, qi will be lost; if it falls short of the count, the qi will not be drained. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right; when the disease resolves, stop. If it does not resolve, needle again according to the method: on the first day of the moon's waxing, one prick; the second day, two pricks, gradually increasing; up to the fifteenth day, fifteen pricks; the sixteenth day, fourteen pricks, gradually decreasing.

When a pathogen lodges in the channel of the foot yang brightness, it causes nosebleed and cold in the upper teeth. Needle at the junction of the second toe's nail with the flesh, one prick on each side. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yang, it causes rib-side pain with inability to breathe freely, coughing, and sweating. Needle at the junction of the fourth toe's nail with the flesh, one prick on each side; the inability to breathe resolves at once, and the sweating stops at once. For coughing, keep warmly clothed and take food and drink, and it will resolve within a day. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right; the disease resolves at once. If it does not resolve, needle again according to the method.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yin, it causes throat pain such that food cannot be taken in, with causeless irritability and a tendency to anger, with qi rushing up to the diaphragm. Needle the vessel at the center of the sole, three pricks on each side, six pricks in all; it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right. When there is swelling within the throat such that food cannot be taken in, and when at times saliva cannot even be spat out, needle in front of Rangu to let out blood, and it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yin, it causes lower back pain radiating into the lower abdomen and pulling at the flank, so that one cannot bend backward or breathe freely. Needle at the juncture of the lower back and sacrum, above the two muscles beside the spine—this is the lumbar transport point—using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks; upon withdrawing the needle it resolves at once. Needle the right for the left, the left for the right.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot greater yang, it causes spasm and tightness of the back, radiating pain into the rib-sides. Needle beginning from the nape, counting down the vertebrae alongside the spine; press firmly, and wherever the response to pressure feels like pain, needle to the side of it, three pricks; it resolves at once.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessel of the foot lesser yang, it causes pain lodging in the hip joint, so that the thigh cannot be raised. Needle the hip joint with a fine needle; if there is cold, retain the needle for a long while, using the waxing and waning of the moon to determine the number of pricks; it resolves at once.

In treating the various channels, if needling their points of passage produces no effect on the disease, then apply crossed needling. For deafness, needle the hand yang brightness; if it does not resolve, needle the connecting vessel that emerges in front of the ear. For dental caries, needle the hand yang brightness; if it does not resolve, needle the vessel that enters the teeth, and it resolves at once. When a pathogen lodges among the five viscera, the disease manifests as pain that pulls along the vessel, coming and going intermittently. Observe where the disease is and apply crossed needling at the nails of the hands and feet; observe the vessel, and let out its blood, needling once every other day; if one needling does not resolve it, five needlings will. When the crossed disease is transmitted to affect the upper teeth, causing cold pain in the teeth and lips, observe the vessel with blood on the back of the hand and remove it; at the foot yang brightness, needle at the nail of the second toe with a great prick, and at the hand, needle above the nail of the index finger, one prick on each; it resolves at once. Take the left for the right, the right for the left.

When a pathogen lodges in the network vessels of the hand and foot lesser yin, the hand and foot greater yin, and the foot yang brightness—these five network vessels all converge within the ear and connect above with the left corner of the head. When all five network vessels are exhausted, it causes all the vessels of the body to stir while the body itself has no awareness; its appearance is like that of a corpse, and it is therefore also called corpse reversal. Needle the inner side of the big toe's nail, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip; afterward needle the center of the sole; then needle above the nail of the middle toe, one prick each. Afterward needle the inner side of the thumb's nail, about the width of a leek leaf from its tip; then needle the heart-governing vessel and the tip of the pisiform bone at the lesser yin, one prick each; it resolves at once. If it does not resolve, use a bamboo tube to blow into both ears, shave a square inch of hair at the left corner, burn and grind it, and have the patient drink it down with a cup of fine wine; for one unable to drink, pour it in—it resolves at once.

In general, the method of needling is: first observe the channel vessels, press and follow along them, examine whether there is deficiency or excess, and regulate accordingly. Where the channel is not in harmony, needle the channel itself; where there is pain but the channel is not diseased, apply crossed needling; and observing wherever there are blood network vessels in the skin regions, remove them entirely. This is the method of crossed needling.
